title: Today's AI Brief: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
summary: A look at the Vatican's unprecedented alliance with Silicon Valley, the challenges of adopting agentic AI, and the chaos caused by AI agents in the tech world
imageAlt: Image from source article for Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageCaption: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageSourceTitle: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation

body.text: I'm looking at the latest news in AI, and one story that caught my eye is the Vatican's invitation to Christopher Olah, cofounder of Anthropic, to speak at the Pope's AI encyclical presentation. This move signals an unprecedented alliance between the Catholic church and Silicon Valley. What's interesting is that the Vatican is recognizing the impact of AI on society and is seeking to understand its potential benefits and risks. I think it's fascinating to see how the Vatican is taking a proactive approach to exploring the intersection of technology and faith.

The Vatican's move is not the only significant development in the AI space. A recent report highlights the challenges organizations face in adopting agentic AI. While 85% of organizations want to be agentic within the next three years, 76% say their current operations and infrastructure can't support that change. This disconnect between ambition and execution is a major hurdle for companies looking to leverage AI. It's like they're trying to build a house without a foundation – it's not going to end well. I'm curious to see how companies will address this gap and what solutions will emerge to support the adoption of agentic AI.

Another story that stands out to me is the chaos caused by AI agents in the tech world. The rise of AI-powered coding tools has led to a new generation of 'addicts' who are swept up in the excitement of creating with AI. However, this enthusiasm has also led to a lack of understanding of the potential risks and consequences of relying on AI agents. It's a reminder that as AI becomes more ubiquitous, we need to be aware of its potential impact on our lives and our work. I've seen it myself – friends who are coders are now spending hours upon hours working with AI tools, and it's like they're in a different world. It's both amazing and a little scary to see how quickly AI is changing the way we work and live.

I'm also looking at the story of Peter Steinberger, who addressed a meetup in London called Claude Code Anonymous. Steinberger and his fellow 'addicts' are seeking to network with others who share their passion for coding with AI. This phenomenon highlights the human side of the AI revolution, where people are coming together to share their experiences and learn from each other. It's like a support group, but instead of overcoming addiction, they're embracing it. I think it's great to see people connecting over their shared interests, and it's a reminder that AI is not just about technology – it's about the people who use it and the communities that form around it.

As I delve deeper into these stories, I'm struck by the complexity and nuance of the AI landscape. It's not just about the technology itself, but about the people, organizations, and societies that are being impacted by it. I think that's what makes AI so fascinating – it's a reflection of our own values, aspirations, and limitations. And as I look at these headlines, I'm reminded that AI is not just a tool, but a mirror that reflects our own humanity.

Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation is also worth a quick mention.
